sections.content:
Technology is where I tend to feel most at ease, which is also where it's easiest to stop questioning yourself.

The Obsidian plugin, no existing plugin for 3D models in notes, so I built one, now has over 2300 installations. That number changes how you work. Version management, community communication, gradual feature rollout, none of that comes up in coursework. It was technically driven: I noticed a problem I had, built a solution, others had the same problem. That worked. But it's a different process than designing for people whose needs you've actually researched, a distinction I became more aware of through the master.

Tactone pushed into different territory, pneumatic actuation, LDR sensor processing, audio modulation, custom silicone pads, all in one coherent system. I tried to make it a reusable platform rather than a one-off: the guide explains how to swap components, upload audio, recalibrate for different environments. Writing that forced a different kind of understanding than just building it. The user study surfaced latency issues I hadn't anticipated, and the setup sequence mattered more than I expected, both pointing to something I keep running into: in physical-digital systems, the seams matter as much as the components.

A thread I want to keep pulling: the gap between technically-driven and human-centered design is something my identity explicitly names as a tension. The direction I want to move is continuing to let both inform each other.
